title: The Towers Decide
subtitle: What DHS says the autonomous AI towers do vs. what the contracts actually authorize
narrative: DHS says the Autonomous Surveillance Towers make the border safer and more efficient. The contracts say something different: machine-decided tracking, autonomous drone dispatch, and no published policy for when the AI decides to escalate.
findings: 890 planned autonomous towers (300+ already deployed); $363M Anduril contract Dec 2025; M2S2 trucks can cue interceptor drones without human approval; RAND study: towers reduce apprehensions 27% (deterrence, not interdiction); Academic study: surveillance rerouting doubles water loss for migrants; Zero federal policy governing AI-to-kinetic escalation decisions.
angle: The automation is not about efficiency — it's about removing human judgment from decisions that end in force. When a tower's AI flags someone and dispatches a drone, who is legally responsible? Nobody knows.
body:
## The Towers Decide

DHS has a pitch for the Autonomous Surveillance Towers: they work around the clock, they don't get tired, they don't get distracted. The border gets safer. Operations get more efficient.

The contracts tell a different story.

---

### What the towers actually do

The Anduril Sentry tower — solar-powered, equipped with radar, LIDAR, electro-optical and infrared cameras — runs AI image recognition on everything it sees. Real-time computer vision. Frame by frame. Trained on millions of images to distinguish people from animals from vehicles.

By late 2024, Anduril had already deployed **300+ towers** covering roughly 30% of the US land border. DHS has a target of **890 total autonomous towers**. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (2025) requires all new border surveillance towers to be certified "autonomous" — a mandate that effectively locks in Anduril as the sole vendor, per The Intercept.

The December 2025 contract: **$362,974,500** for one year, SBIR Phase III IDIQ. One contractor. No competition for the core spec.

The M2S2 trucks — Modular Mobile Surveillance System — go further. Rolling AI-powered observation towers with detection ranges of several miles. They can be deployed in minutes. The procurement documents specify two modes: agent-present (operator on site) and **unattended/autonomous** — onboard AI conducts surveillance and sends alerts to remote operators.

The unattended mode is the point.

---

### The drone dispatch question

M2S2 procurement documents, as covered by WIRED and GovConWire, explicitly state the system may be designed to **"cue other DHS assets, including electronic warfare systems, kinetic systems (e.g., interceptor drones)."**

Read that again: a machine, running AI, watching a miles-long corridor, can trigger a drone intercept.

Not "can be configured to." Not "has a mode for." The documents frame it as a designed capability.

CBP Office of Field Operations already has a drone fleet. May 2026 social media posts from CBP show drone integration with surveillance towers. The architecture is being built out.

**No federal policy governs when an autonomous tower's AI decision escalates to a drone intercept.** There is no published protocol. No congressional authorization for the specific kinetic pathway described in the procurement docs. The machine decides, and the legal structure around that decision is, at minimum, ambiguous.

---

### What the RAND study actually found

DHS claims the towers improve border security outcomes. The RAND Corporation published a sponsored study in 2020: *"Modeling the Impact of Border-Enforcement Measures."*

The findings on Integrated Fixed Towers (the predecessor to today's autonomous systems): **27% fewer apprehensions** in treated zones.

Not more interdictions. Fewer. The dominant effect is deterrence — migrants walk around the viewshed. They don't walk into it.

For Tactical Aerostats (tethered radar balloons), the numbers were different: apprehensions increased 26–32%. Improved detection dominated. But those are a different system.

The implication for today's autonomous towers: if the goal is actually reducing successful illegal border crossings, the evidence is mixed at best. If the goal is surveillance coverage and deterrence — pushing crossings to more dangerous routes — the towers are effective.

A 2025 study in *Political Geography* confirmed the mechanism: crossers avoiding surveillance zones suffer **double the water loss**. They're forced through more arid, more lethal terrain. The surveillance technology produces bodily harm as a geopolitical side effect. The RAND findings on reduced crossing success are consistent with this — the people who can't go around are dying in the attempt.

---

### The accountability gap

DHS's own AI Use Case Inventory lists multiple deployed autonomous systems, including the Tethered Aerostat Radar System — automates sensor operations with AI image analysis correlated with AIS maritime data.

The inventory exists. No policy framework governs when autonomous detection becomes autonomous action.

The CBP Commissioner said four vendors have passed the "autonomy test." What that test consists of — what threshold triggers a flag, what weight the AI assigns to behavioral patterns versus biometric match versus proximity to a vehicle — is not public.

The system watches. The system decides. The system sometimes dispatches.

**Nobody has published the decision tree.**

---

### What officials say vs. what the contracts authorize

| What officials say | What the contracts say |
|---|---|
| Towers make the border more efficient | Sole-source $363M contract awarded to Anduril |
| Autonomous means around-the-clock coverage | Autonomous means AI conducts detection and reporting without human in loop |
| Towers enhance officer safety | M2S2 unattended mode deploys with no officer present; sends alerts remotely |
| Escalation requires human judgment | M2S2 procurement: system "cues" drones and EW systems |
| Towers deter crossings | RAND: 27% fewer apprehensions in tower zones (deterrence, not interdiction) |

---

### The verdict

The autonomous surveillance tower program is not primarily about efficiency. The efficiency metrics — apprehensions, successful interdiction — show mixed or negative results in the academic literature. The program is about autonomous surveillance coverage at scale, with a designed but unregulated pathway to kinetic escalation.

When the AI flags a figure in the viewshed and a drone is dispatched, the legal question of who authorized that decision has no clear answer in public record.

DHS wants the towers. The towers decide.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The autonomous tower program is not about efficiency — it's about removing human judgment from escalation decisions. The drone dispatch pathway is designed but ungoverned. When the tower decides, nobody is legally on the hook.
